questions.question: Define a signal in Unix System Programming and explain its fundamental characteristics.
keyPoints: Signal is a software interrupt delivered by kernel to process
Notifies process about asynchronous event occurrence
Limited form of inter-process communication (IPC)
Can be generated by kernel, processes, or terminal
Carries minimal information (just signal number)

questions.question: Explain the three possible dispositions (actions) for handling a signal in Unix.
keyPoints: Default action: kernel takes predefined action (terminate, core dump, stop, continue)
Ignore the signal: signal is discarded, process continues normally
Catch the signal: user-defined handler function is executed

questions.question: Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous signals with examples.
keyPoints: Synchronous: generated in direct response to process execution (SIGSEGV, SIGFPE, SIGBUS)
Asynchronous: generated by external events (SIGINT, SIGALRM, SIGHUP)
Synchronous delivered immediately at point of occurrence
Asynchronous delivered at any time during process execution

questions.question: What is a signal mask and how does it affect signal delivery?
keyPoints: Signal mask is a set of blocked signals for a process
Blocked signals cannot be delivered immediately
Blocked signals become pending
Pending signals are delivered when unblocked
Each process has its own signal mask maintained by kernel

questions.question: Explain the purpose and behavior of SIGCHLD signal in Unix.
keyPoints: Sent to parent when child terminates, stops, or continues
By default, parent ignores this signal
If set to SIG_IGN, child processes are automatically reaped (no zombie)
Used with wait()/waitpid() to collect child exit status

questions.question: Explain the difference between SIGTERM and SIGKILL signals.
keyPoints: SIGTERM (15): can be caught, ignored, or blocked - graceful termination
SIGKILL (9): cannot be caught, ignored, or blocked - immediate termination
SIGTERM allows process to clean up (close files, release resources)
SIGKILL is used when process is unresponsive or cannot terminate gracefully

questions.question: What are pending signals in Unix and how are they handled?
keyPoints: Pending signals are generated but blocked from delivery
Kernel maintains a pending signal set for each process
For standard signals, multiple occurrences are merged into one
For real-time signals, all occurrences are queued
Delivered when process unblocks the signal

questions.question: Explain the complete signal handling mechanism in Unix, covering signal generation, delivery, and action. Discuss signal disposition and common signal types.
keyPoints: Signal generation: kernel creates signal due to hardware exception, terminal event, or kill() system call
Signal delivery: kernel checks process signal mask - if blocked, signal becomes pending; if not blocked, delivered
Signal action: based on disposition - default (terminate/stop/continue/core dump), ignore, or catch with handler
Signal disposition: set via signal() or sigaction() system calls
Common signals: SIGKILL(9), SIGTERM(15), SIGINT(2), SIGHUP(1), SIGCHLD(17), SIGSEGV(11), SIGALRM(14)

questions.question: Discuss real-time signals and their advantages over standard signals in Unix. Also explain the concept of signal handler reliability.
keyPoints: Real-time signals: SIGRTMIN to SIGRTMAX range
Queued: each occurrence is delivered (no merging)
Defined ordering: lower signal number has higher priority
Can carry additional information via siginfo_t
Reliable signals: handlers persist across deliveries (unlike early Unix)
Signal masking prevents signal interruption

questions.question: Write a detailed note on signal handling in daemon processes and explain how SIGHUP is used for configuration reloading.
keyPoints: Daemons are background processes detached from terminal
They ignore SIGHUP to survive terminal closure
Common pattern: catch SIGHUP to reload configuration files
Process opens config, parses it, applies new settings without restart
Useful for long-running services (web servers, network daemons)
Prevents service disruption during configuration changes
